title: Modeling and Resource Optimization for Quantum Oracles
authors: Zhihang Li, Bo Zhao, Chuanbing Han, Jie Zhao, Jinchen Xu, Guoqiang Shu, Yimin Gao, Woji He, Zheng Shan
abstract: Quantum computing has demonstrated its significant advantage over supercomputing for specific applications and shown promising prospect, such as machine learning, cryptography, finance, etc.. Quantum oracles are very common in many quantum algorithms and oracle resource consumption directly affects algorithm performance. However, existing oracle designs often exhibit high resource overhead and limited compatibility. Moreover, structured description tools and complexity analysis methods are lacked. In this work, we introduces a Hierarchical Recursive Synthesis-Evaluation (HRSE) model, enabling formal description and precise quantum gate complexity analysis of oracles. Based on this model, we propose an Adaptive Space-depth Trade-off (ASDT) algorithm for generating oracle structures under a fixed qubit constraint. We provide a theoretical proof showing that the ASDT algorithm achieves the optimal gate count for a given number of qubits. Experimental results show that the ASDT algorithm reduces the average quantum circuit depth by 53.99% compared with the W-cycle approach, with the number of variables being 10, 15, and 20, respectively.
summary: This paper introduces a new framework and algorithm for designing more efficient quantum oracles. By using a hierarchical recursive model, the authors can systematically balance the use of auxiliary qubits against circuit depth, significantly reducing the computational cost of essential quantum algorithms.
main_problem: The optimization of quantum oracle construction to reduce resource overhead, specifically minimizing quantum gate complexity and circuit depth while managing the trade-off between qubit count and depth.
main_result: The proposed ASDT algorithm is theoretically proven to achieve optimal gate counts for a given number of qubits and experimentally reduces average quantum circuit depth by 53.99% compared to the W-cycle approach.
method: The authors introduce the Hierarchical Recursive Synthesis-Evaluation (HRSE) model to formally describe oracle architectures and the Adaptive Space-depth Trade-off (ASDT) algorithm to optimize their structure.
model_or_system: The work focuses on Boolean functions f: {0, 1}^n -> {0, 1} represented as quantum oracles, using a hierarchical tree-based model (HRSE) where nodes represent computation units and edges represent recursive containment.
key_observables: Quantum gate complexity (gate count), quantum circuit depth, and the optimization ratio compared to baseline methods.
important_parameters: Number of variables (n), number of auxiliary qubits (k/m), recursion depth (level), and the trade-off between space (qubits) and depth (time).
main_assumptions: The model assumes the monotonicity of node size and that nodes with size 1 or 2 must be leaf nodes; it also focuses on the organization of functions rather than specific function encoding.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows oracle conversion and qubit multiplexing; Figure 2 illustrates the HRSE model; Figure 3 plots node depth vs. function count; Figure 4 shows the ASDT algorithm execution; Figure 5 displays the space-depth trade-off bar graph.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of oracle overhead, presents the HRSE mathematical model, details the ASDT algorithm and its optimality proof, provides experimental comparisons against the W-cycle approach, and discusses future optimization directions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Framework for constructing quantum oracles using the qubit mul- tiplexing technique. (a) Conversion between XOR and phase oracles using multi-controlled X (MCX) and Z (MCZ) gates. (b) Evaluation of three constraint functions via qubit multiplexing. Each function is first encoded into an auxiliary qubit. The results are then merged onto a target qubit using an MCX gate, after which the auxiliary qubits are reset for reuse. (c) Hierarchical composition of single-function and multi-function oracles, where smaller modules are recursively combined into higher-level structures, thereby reducing the required number of auxiliary qubits.
Fig. 2. An illustrative example of the HRSE model representing an oracle structure with 4 constraint functions and 4 auxiliary qubits (m = 4, k = 4).
Fig. 3. Average node depth versus the number of constraint functions for different auxiliary qubit counts. The curves represent results for auxiliary qubit counts k ranging from 6 to 10.

Fig. 4. Incremental construction of the HRSE tree Tk(m) using the ASDT algorithm, illustrated with a small problem instance. At each step, the parent node selected for expansion by the ASDT strategy is highlighted in red, and the newly added child node is highlighted in blue. The iterative process continues until the tree contains 7 leaf nodes, corresponding to the 7 constraint functions to be implemented.

Fig. 5. Space-depth trade-off of the ASDT algorithm. Bar groups correspond to different numbers of variables, while colors represent the number of auxiliary qubits. The experimental results demonstrate how the circuit depth decreases as more auxiliary qubits (i.e., greater space cost) are allocated, illustrating the trade-off between circuit depth and qubit count.
